title: 🎙️ Science, Wonder & the Natural World
description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Science, Wonder & the Natural World' — scientific discovery, ecology, natural phenomena, and the curiosity-driven study of living systems. Strong fits include: wildlife biology and species research, ecology studies and biodiversity findings, watershed and salmon research relevant to BC, astronomy and space science, new species discoveries, citizen science projects, natural history stories, wildfire science and forest ecology, climate science findings (not just policy), geology, evolution, and deep-time Earth stories, and any scientific research grounded in BC or Cariboo landscapes and ecosystems. Prefer articles that convey genuine curiosity and discovery — the 'how' and 'why' of the natural world. Score 70–90 for: prehistoric life and palaeontology (ancient oceans, Cretaceous ecology, deep-time species), new species discoveries in ocean or remote environments, deep-sea biology, evolutionary biology findings, natural history stories with a strong sense of wonder, and field research grounded in BC ecosystems. Penalise (score 0–30): consumer health and pharmaceutical research without ecology or natural-world context; pure policy and advocacy news; technology business stories without a science or ecology angle; human interest and viral news without scientific substance; geopolitical and crime news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Future moon landings could wipe out clues to how life began on Earth
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

A new study suggests spacecraft exhaust could quickly contaminate the moon's most scientifically valuable regions, potentially masking ancient clues about how life began on Earth. Researchers say future lunar missions should consider new ways to reduce and monitor this pollution before it becomes widespread.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists finally solved the mystery of Earth's greatest mass extinction
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Why do beaches today have seashells from clams and snails instead of brachiopods? A new study suggests the answer lies in Earth's greatest mass extinction, when warming oceans and falling oxygen levels wiped out animals that couldn't adapt. Species with body plans and metabolisms better suited to the changing conditions survived and went on to dominate the seas, offering a glimpse of how modern marine life could respond to climate change.

items.title: [Boing Boing] UK politician Ann Widdecombe beaten to death in own home
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

British politician and novelist Ann Widdecombe, a former minister of prisons known for her dogmatic conservative views, was found dead Thursday at her home in Devon, England. Long out of parliament and 78 years old, it was initially assumed she had died naturally, but it soon emerged she was beaten to death and that police had arrested a 26 year-old man on suspicion of her murder. — Read the rest

The post UK politician Ann Widdecombe beaten to death in own home appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Passenger nearly pulled out a Ryanair window in mid-air
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

A window came off a Ryanair jet mid-flight over North Macedonia on Thursday, and a passenger was partly pulled through the hole before others hauled him back by his seat belt. The flight from Thessaloniki to Memmingen turned back after debris — Greek media said it came off an engine — struck and detached one of the cabin windows, decompressing the plane and dropping the oxygen masks. — Read the rest

The post Passenger nearly pulled out a Ryanair window in mid-air appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [New Atlas] Ovaries take on a surprising new job after menopause, study reveals
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

The ovaries are the earliest aging organ. Their supply of egg-containing follicles slowly runs out over a woman's lifetime, so that by the time menopause arrives, follicle numbers have dwindled to approximately one thousand.

Continue Reading

items.title: [Boing Boing] A bison tossed a Yellowstone tourist 8 feet in the air
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

A man walking with his grandson at Yellowstone's Bridge Bay Campground was hurled about eight feet into the air by a bull bison on July 10, Cowboy State Daily reports. The two believed they were keeping a safe distance — more than 100 yards — when a passing white pickup truck spooked the animal into charging, and it turned on the pair as they ducked into nearby trees. — Read the rest

The post A bison tossed a Yellowstone tourist 8 feet in the air appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists discover how the brain rewires itself to truly multitask
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Practice may do more than make perfect. Researchers found that extensive training physically reorganizes the brain, allowing learned tasks to bypass the prefrontal cortex and run through specialized circuits instead. By freeing the brain's "thinking" center, people became better at performing another task at the same time, challenging the long-held idea that humans only switch rapidly between tasks rather than truly multitask.
items.summary: Practice may do more than make perfect. Researchers found that extensive training physically reorganizes the brain, allowing learned tasks to bypass the prefrontal cortex and run through specialized circuits instead. By freeing the brain's "thinking" center, people became better at performing

items.title: [MacRumors] Two Apple Stores in U.S. Are Moving Soon
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Two of Apple's retail stores in the U.S. will be relocating later this month, although they will only be a short distance from their existing locations.

Apple Renaissance at Colony Park

Apple Queens Center in Elmhurst, New York is moving to a temporary location in the shopping mall on Friday, July 17 at 10 a.m. local time, presumably to allow for renovations to be completed at the existing store over the coming months.

Apple Renaissance at Colony Park in Ridgeland, Mississippi is moving to a new spot within the same shopping center on Friday, July 24 at 10 a.m. local time. Apple's new storefront will be located right in front of the Show Fountain.
Tag: Apple Store

This article, "Two Apple Stores in U.S. Are Moving Soon" first appeared on MacRumors.com

Discuss this article in our forums
